perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure; 3.1.2 Is independently developed by the Co mpany provided that party can show that such development was accomp lished without the use of or any reference to the Confidential Information; 3.1.3 Becomes known to the Com pany from another source without confidentiality restriction on subsequent disclosure or use, provided that such source is not in breach of its obligations of non-disclosure; 3.1.4 Is or becomes a par t of the public domain thr ough no wrongful act of the Company; or 3.1.5 Is subsequently publicly disclosed by the TTC.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure, however the Company must immediately inform the TTC; 3.1.2 Is independently developed by the Co mpany provided that party can show that such development was accomp lished without the use of or any reference to the Confidential Information; 3.1.3 Becomes known to the Com pany from another source without confidentiality restriction on subsequent disclosure or use, provided that such source is not in breach of its obligations of non-disclosure; 3.1.4 Is or becomes a par t of the public domain thr ough no wrongful act of the Company; or 3.1.5 Is subsequently publicly disclosed by the TTC.
perturbation.explanation: Originally, clause 3.1.1 stated the Company's confidentiality obligations do not apply if the Confidential Information is already known to the Company at the time of disclosure. The modified version adds a requirement for the Company to immediately inform the TTC if this occurs. This addition creates an in-text contradiction because the original clause implies no further action is needed by the Company if the information was already known. Now, this requires the company to inform the TTC, contradicting the initial statement that confidentiality obligations do not apply, which introduces ambiguity. Clause 3.1 now contains an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure, however the Company must immediately inform the TTC; 3.1.2 Is independently developed by the Co mpany provided that party can show that such development was accomp lished without the use of or any reference to the Confidential Information; 3.1.3 Becomes known to the Com pany from another source without confidentiality restriction on subsequent disclosure or use, provided that such source is not in breach of its obligations of non-disclosure; 3.1.4 Is or becomes a par t of the public domain thr ough no wrongful act of the Company; or 3.1.5 Is subsequently publicly disclosed by the TTC.
perturbation.justification: The original clause 3.1.1 stated that the Company's confidentiality obligations do not apply if the Confidential Information is already known to the Company at the time of disclosure. The modified version adds the requirement for the Company to immediately inform the TTC if this occurs. This creates a direct conflict because the original clause implies no further action is needed by the Company. Now the company must inform the TTC, contradicting the initial statement that confidentiality obligations do not apply, as the notification now constitutes a form of obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1.3 A term or condition of this Agr eement can be waived or modified only by written consent of both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1.3 A term or condition of this Agr eement can be waived or modified only by written consent of both parties, except that the Chief Revenue Operations Officer of the TTC can unilaterally modify Schedule A.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 6.1.3 originally states that any modification to the agreement requires written consent from both parties. The modified version introduces an exception, allowing the Chief Revenue Operations Officer of the TTC to unilaterally modify Schedule A. This creates an in-text contradiction: the original clause requires mutual agreement for any modification, while the changed text allows for unilateral modification by one party for a specific part of the agreement. Now, the contract contains an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1.3 A term or condition of this Agr eement can be waived or modified only by written consent of both parties, except that the Chief Revenue Operations Officer of the TTC can unilaterally modify Schedule A.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that any modification requires written consent from both parties. The changed text introduces an exception, allowing the Chief Revenue Operations Officer to unilaterally modify Schedule A. This directly contradicts the original clause, as it allows for unilateral modification, while the original text mandates mutual agreement for any modification. The contradiction lies in the fact that the original text requires both parties to agree on modifications, while the changed text allows one party to modify Schedule A without the other's consent, which is a direct conflict and fits the 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1.9 Both parties’ obligatio ns of confidentiality sha ll continue for a period of three (3) years following the last discl osure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1.9 Both parties’ obligatio ns of confidentiality sha ll continue for a period of three (3) years following the last discl osure of Confidential Information, unless terminated earlier by the TTC upon written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause 6.1.9 states that confidentiality obligations last for three years after the last disclosure of confidential information. The modified version adds a caveat that the TTC can terminate these obligations earlier with written notice. This creates an in-text contradiction: the original clause suggests a fixed term of three years, while the new version allows for early termination, making the duration uncertain. This leads to a In-Text contradiction within Clause 6, between the duration of confidentiality and right to termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1.9 Both parties’ obligatio ns of confidentiality sha ll continue for a period of three (3) years following the last discl osure of Confidential Information, unless terminated earlier by the TTC upon written notice.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in clause 6.1.9 introduces a contradiction. The original text specifies a fixed confidentiality period of three years. The changed text adds an exception allowing the TTC to terminate the confidentiality earlier. This creates a direct conflict and uncertainty regarding the actual duration of the confidentiality obligation, as the three-year term is no longer guaranteed. Therefore, the changed text contradicts itself in the same clause by first specifying a period of three years and then allowing termination within that period. This falls under Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction, as the key term (duration of confidentiality) is defined inconsistently within the same clause, making its interpretation unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Unless a written agr eement with respect to the Purpose is entered into by the Parties (and except for the matters spec ifically agreed to herein), neither party shall be under any legal obligation with re spect to the Purpose or otherwise by virtue of this Agreement or any written or oral expression with respect to the Purpose by any of their respective dire ctors, officers, employees or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Unless a written agr eement with respect to the Purpose is entered into by the Parties (and except for the matters spec ifically agreed to herein), neither party shall be under any legal obligation with re spect to the Purpose or otherwise by virtue of this Agreement, except for the obligation to negotiate in good faith towards a final agreement, or any written or oral expression with respect to the Purpose by any of their respective dire ctors, officers, employees or agents.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause 5.1 states that neither party is under any legal obligation with respect to the Purpose of the agreement unless a further written agreement is made. The modified version adds an exception that both parties are obligated to negotiate in good faith towards a final agreement. This creates an in-text contradiction: the original text implies no obligation, while the modified version introduces an obligation to negotiate, thus an in-text contradiction arises within clause 5.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 Unless a written agr eement with respect to the Purpose is entered into by the Parties (and except for the matters spec ifically agreed to herein), neither party shall be under any legal obligation with re spect to the Purpose or otherwise by virtue of this Agreement, except for the obligation to negotiate in good faith towards a final agreement, or any written or oral expression with respect to the Purpose by any of their respective dire ctors, officers, employees or agents.
perturbation.justification: The original clause states that neither party has any legal obligation unless a written agreement is made. The changed version introduces an exception, obligating both parties to negotiate in good faith. This creates a direct contradiction, as the clause initially denies any obligation but then imposes one. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text, but the explanation states that the introduction of the negotiation in good faith clause directly contradicts the initial intent of no obligation.
